findings.description: allowUnknownProtocols defaults to true, allowing dangerous URIs with unknown protocols in HTML attributes
findings.reasoning: The `isAllowUnknownProtocols()` method defaults to `Boolean.TRUE`. In `SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.isAllowedValue()`, when `allowUnknownProtocols` is true (the default), any URL value that doesn't match the `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA` pattern (`^(?:\\w+script|data):`) is allowed. This means protocols like `vbscript:` (if the word doesn't end in 'script', note 'vbscript' does end in script so is blocked), but more critically protocols like `blob:`, `filesystem:`, or any custom/uncommon protocol would be allowed through. For example, `blob:http://attacker.com/uuid` could be allowed in href attributes. The only thing blocking `javascript:` and `data:` URIs via this path is the `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA` regex, but since the default allows unknown protocols, any protocol not explicitly in the allowed list or blocked list passes through. This creates a significant security risk since the sanitizer is named "secure" and is the default implementation.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTMLElementSanitizerConfiguration.isAllowUnknownProtocols() returns Boolean.TRUE by default -> SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.initialize() sets this.allowUnknownProtocols=true -> isAllowedValue() uses this.allowUnknownProtocols to bypass protocol checking for any protocol not matching IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA
evidence.notes: The test file explicitly sets allowUnknownProtocols=false in tests, confirming this is a known concern. The production default is TRUE which is the opposite of what a 'secure' sanitizer should do.

exploit_payload.code_snippet: Click me Click me Click
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker injects HTML with custom/unknown protocol URIs that bypass the SecureHTMLElementSanitizer because allowUnknownProtocols defaults to true; only javascript: and data: (and *script:) are blocked
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to supply HTML content that goes through the XWiki HTML cleaner with default sanitizer configuration

findings.description: isAllowedDataValue permits any data: URI MIME type for img/audio/video/source elements, including data:text/html which can execute JavaScript
findings.reasoning: The `isAllowedDataValue` method allows any `data:` URI in `src`, `xlink:href`, or `href` attributes of elements in `dataUriTags` (audio, video, img, source, image, track). There is NO check on the MIME type of the data URI. This means `data:text/html,<script>alert(1)</script>` would be allowed in `src` attributes of `img`, `audio`, `video`, `source`, `image`, and `track` elements. While `data:text/html` in an img src is typically not executed by browsers, `data:text/html` in a link href, or `data:text/javascript` in any of these elements, could lead to XSS in certain browser/context combinations. Additionally, `data:application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and `data:text/html` in iframes (if iframe were added to dataUriTags via config) would be dangerous. The `attributeValue.startsWith("data:")` check is also NOT normalized — it doesn't strip whitespace first (unlike other checks in the code that use `valueNoWhitespace`), making it potentially bypassable with whitespace before `data:` since `startsWith` check is on the raw `attributeValue`.

evidence.notes: The key issue is: 1) No MIME type validation on data: URIs - data:text/html and data:text/javascript are allowed for img/audio/video/source. 2) Uses raw attributeValue not whitespace-stripped version - whitespace before 'data:' could bypass startsWith check in some browsers that strip leading whitespace from attribute values before parsing.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker injects HTML with data: URI of dangerous MIME types in src attribute of img/audio/video/source elements; the sanitizer allows any data: URI for these elements without checking MIME type
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to supply HTML content that goes through the XWiki HTML cleaner/sanitizer
exploit_payload.category: XSS via unrestricted data: URI MIME type

findings.description: SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.isAllowedValue() allows unknown protocols by default when allowUnknownProtocols is true (the default), effectively bypassing the secure URI allowlist
findings.reasoning: In `isAllowedValue()` at line 206, when `this.allowUnknownProtocols` is `true` (which is the default per `HTMLElementSanitizerConfiguration.isAllowUnknownProtocols()` returning `Boolean.TRUE`), any attribute value that does NOT match `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA` (`^(?:\\w+script|data):`) is allowed. This completely bypasses the explicit allowlist-based URI protocol check (`IS_ALLOWED_URI`) for any URL not matching the blocked pattern. The IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA regex only blocks patterns matching `*script:` (like `javascript:`, `vbscript:`) and `data:`. This means protocols like `blob:`, `filesystem:`, `intent:`, custom app protocols, etc., are all allowed through by default. The secure sanitizer's name implies it should be secure by default, but `allowUnknownProtocols=true` makes it behave as an insecure sanitizer for URL protocol validation.

evidence.notes: The test explicitly sets allowUnknownProtocols=false to test the secure behavior. The production default is TRUE which defeats the purpose of the secure sanitizer for URL protocol checking. This contradicts the security intent of the class.

exploit_payload.code_snippet: Click Click Click
exploit_payload.scenario: Default SecureHTMLElementSanitizer configuration allows any unknown URI protocol (not javascript:/vbscript:/data:) through href/src attributes, enabling protocol-based attacks on users
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Default XWiki configuration - no special setup required; attacker needs to submit HTML content to XWiki

findings.description: InsecureHTMLElementSanitizer allows all HTML elements and attributes unconditionally, enabling XSS when used as the active sanitizer
findings.reasoning: The `InsecureHTMLElementSanitizer` returns `true` for all `isElementAllowed()` and `isAttributeAllowed()` calls, meaning it allows every element (including `script`, `style`) and every attribute (including `onerror`, `onload`, `onclick`, event handlers) without any restriction. This sanitizer is registered with the hint "insecure" and can be activated via the `xml.htmlElementSanitizer` configuration key or via the execution context hint (`HTMLElementSanitizer.EXECUTION_CONTEXT_HINT_KEY`). The `DefaultHTMLElementSanitizer.getImplementation()` loads the sanitizer by hint from the execution context at runtime, allowing any code that can write to the execution context to switch to the insecure sanitizer. If an attacker or privileged-but-not-admin user can set the execution context hint to "insecure", all HTML sanitization would be bypassed.

evidence.notes: While this is named 'insecure' by design, it can be activated dynamically via execution context. The real risk is if anything in the codebase sets EXECUTION_CONTEXT_HINT_KEY to 'insecure' in a code path that processes untrusted HTML.

exploit_payload.scenario: A user with script execution rights sets the execution context sanitizer hint to 'insecure', causing all subsequent HTML processing to bypass sanitization and allow arbitrary XSS payloads
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to set execution context properties (typically requires script programming rights in XWiki)

findings.description: LinkFilter noreferrer/noopener fields - false positive, these are security-adding constants not vulnerabilities
findings.code_snippet:
private static final String NOOPENER = "noopener";

    private static final String NOREFERRER = "noreferrer";
findings.reasoning: The CodeQL query flagged `noreferrer` and `noopener` as potential XSS bypass issues in LinkFilter. However, these constants are used DEFENSIVELY - the LinkFilter actively ADDS `noopener` and `noreferrer` to the `rel` attribute of external links that open in new windows/frames. This is a security hardening measure to prevent reverse tabnapping attacks, not a vulnerability. The filter correctly adds these values to protect against window.opener exploitation.

evidence.notes: This is a defensive security measure, not a vulnerability. The constants are used to add security-relevant rel attributes to external links.

findings.description: LinkFilter.filter() contains() checks for noopener/noreferrer - false positive, these are defensive checks before adding security attributes
findings.reasoning: The CodeQL query flagged these `contains()` calls as potentially insufficient URL/string checks. However, these are NOT URL protocol validation checks at all - they are simple list membership checks to avoid adding duplicate `noopener`/`noreferrer` values to the `rel` attribute list. They are part of a security hardening feature that adds these rel attributes to prevent reverse tabnapping. There is no vulnerability here.

evidence.code_context: The filter iterates external links and adds rel='noopener noreferrer' to prevent window.opener exploitation. The contains() calls are idempotency checks - they prevent adding the same rel value twice, not URL protocol validation.
evidence.notes: False positive - these are list.contains() checks on rel attribute values, not URL protocol checks.

findings.description: IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA, IS_ALLOWED_URI patterns - false positive, CASE_INSENSITIVE flag is correctly applied for relevant patterns
findings.reasoning: Query 13 (20260403111957) flags Pattern.compile calls at lines 66, 68, 70 without CASE_INSENSITIVE. However: (1) ATTR_WHITESPACE at line 64 matches Unicode whitespace characters - case sensitivity is irrelevant for Unicode codepoints. (2) DATA_ATTR at line 66 and ARIA_ATTR at line 68 match HTML attribute names which are already lowercased before matching (line 182: `String lowerAttribute = attributeName.toLowerCase()`). (3) IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA at line 63 and IS_ALLOWED_URI at line 72 correctly use CASE_INSENSITIVE. Query 14 (20260403112102) confirms the critical patterns have CASE_INSENSITIVE. Query 12 (20260403111910) about IS_BLOCKED_URI lowercase is also a false positive since the code already lowercases via `toLowerCase()` before checking. The patterns without CASE_INSENSITIVE are used on already-lowercased input.

evidence.notes: The critical security patterns (IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA, IS_ALLOWED_URI) correctly use CASE_INSENSITIVE. The remaining patterns are applied to already-normalized (lowercased) input. No bypass is possible.

findings.description: SanitizerFilter namespace/SVG string constants and methods - false positive, these are defensive namespace validation checks
findings.reasoning: Query 4 (20260403105926) flags namespace string constants and tag name strings ('math', 'svg', 'html') in SanitizerFilter. These are used for namespace validation in `checkNamespace()` and `traverseWithNamespace()` to prevent namespace confusion attacks (where SVG/MathML elements could be used to bypass HTML sanitization). The namespace checking logic actually PREVENTS XSS by ensuring elements are only allowed in their correct namespace context. The string comparisons like `"math".equals(element.getTagName())` and `"svg".equals(element.getTagName())` are exact string equality checks that cannot be bypassed. Similarly, the `"true"` string at line 79 is the value for the RESTRICTED parameter check.

evidence.notes: These are defensive security checks, not vulnerabilities. The SanitizerFilter follows DOMPurify's approach for namespace handling.

findings.description: SecureHTMLElementSanitizer URL/string checks in isElementAllowed, isAttributeAllowed, isAllowedValue - false positives for properly implemented allowlist/blocklist checks
findings.reasoning: Queries 2 (20260403105410), 8 (20260403110950), 9 (20260403111308), and 11 (20260403111735) flag various contains()/toLowerCase()/matcher() calls in isElementAllowed, isAttributeAllowed, and isAllowedValue. These are all part of the proper allowlist-based sanitization logic: (1) `contains()` checks on forbidTags/extraAllowedTags/forbidAttributes are exact string set membership checks - not bypassable. (2) `toLowerCase()` is correctly applied before pattern matching to normalize case. (3) The `IS_ALLOWED_URI` and `IS_NO_URI` patterns use CASE_INSENSITIVE. (4) The `getAllowedUriRegexp()` configuration method is used to compile a custom pattern with CASE_INSENSITIVE flag at line 160. These are all correctly implemented - the real issue is the `allowUnknownProtocols=true` default already reported as a separate finding.

evidence.notes: These are properly implemented string checks. The real vulnerability is allowUnknownProtocols=true default, already reported separately.

findings.description: EscapeTool string constants flagged as XSS - false positive
findings.reasoning:
The CodeQL query flagged string constants used within the EscapeTool class itself - a utility class that provides proper HTML/XML/JSON/URL/CSS escaping. The flagged lines are:
- Line 59: `DEFAULT_KEY = "escapetool"` - a key name constant
- Line 64: `EQUALS = "="` - URL query string separator constant
- Line 67: `AND = "&"` - URL query string separator constant
- Line 219: empty string in css() fallback return
- Line 244: error message string in css() exception handler
- Line 265-272: URL encoding constants ("UTF-8", error message, "\\+", "%20") in url() encoding method

None of these are XSS sinks. The class itself provides encoding methods (xml(), json(), url(), css()) that properly escape user input before output. These are implementation details of the escape functions, not vulnerabilities.

evidence.code_context: EscapeTool extends velocity's EscapeTool and provides xml(), json(), url(), css() methods using XMLUtils.escape(), StringEscapeUtils.escapeJson(), URLEncoder.encode(), and CSSIdentifierSerializer. All flagged lines are string constants within the encoding implementation.
evidence.notes: False positive - CodeQL flagged string literals within an escaping utility class

findings.description: HTMLImageEmbedder data URI handling flagged as XSS - false positive
findings.reasoning: The HTMLImageEmbedder is an XMLDiffFilter that converts image src attributes to data URIs (base64-encoded image content) before computing HTML diffs, then restores them afterward. The flagged string constants are component names, attribute names, and user data keys - not XSS sinks. The data URI is placed in an `` attribute. Modern browsers do not execute JavaScript from data URIs in img src attributes. The `SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.isAllowedDataValue()` explicitly allows `data:` URIs for img src attributes, which is the expected behavior for image embedding. The warning log at line 110 uses ExceptionUtils.getRootCauseMessage() which only returns a message string, not user-controlled content directly injected into HTML output. This is by design for the diff feature and does not create an XSS vulnerability path.

evidence.code_context: HTMLImageEmbedder converts image src URLs to base64 data URIs before diff computation and restores them after. The data URI goes into img src attribute only. The SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.isAllowedDataValue() method explicitly whitelists data: URIs for img src.
evidence.notes: False positive - string constants and data URI handling for img elements, which is XSS-safe in modern browsers

findings.description: ControlCharactersFilter component name constant flagged as XSS - false positive
findings.reasoning:
The CodeQL query flagged line 44 which contains the component hint string `"controlcharacters"` in the `@Named` annotation. This is a component registration name, not an XSS sink.

The ControlCharactersFilter itself filters text nodes in the DOM, replacing ASCII control characters (0x00-0x1F range, excluding newlines 10, 13) with spaces or the Unicode replacement character U+FFFD. It only processes `Text` DOM nodes (not elements or attributes), so it cannot create XSS. It also only filters outward-bound rendered characters from already-parsed HTML, not injecting user content into output.

The concern about the filter not stripping all XSS-relevant characters is also not applicable because text nodes cannot contain executable HTML - they are always text-encoded when serialized back to HTML.

evidence.code_context: ControlCharactersFilter processes Text DOM nodes, replacing control chars (1-31 except 10,13) with spaces and null char with U+FFFD. The @Named('controlcharacters') annotation at line 44 is just a component registration name. Text nodes are safely encoded when serialized to HTML.
evidence.notes: False positive - @Named annotation string constant, not an XSS sink. The filter itself is correct for its purpose.

findings.description: HTMLElementSanitizerConfiguration.isAllowUnknownProtocols() defaults to Boolean.TRUE, potentially allowing non-standard URI protocols in sanitized HTML
findings.reasoning:
The `isAllowUnknownProtocols()` method returns `Boolean.TRUE` as the default value when no configuration is present. This value is used in `SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.isAllowedValue()` at line 206:

```java
result = result || (this.allowUnknownProtocols && !isScriptOrData(attributeValue));
```

The `isScriptOrData()` check uses `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA = Pattern.compile("^(?:\\w+script|data):", Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE)` which blocks patterns ending in "script" (e.g., `javascript:`, `vbscript:`) and `data:`.

However, with `allowUnknownProtocols=true` (the default), any URI that doesn't match IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA AND doesn't match IS_NO_URI would be allowed. This could include protocols like:
- `blob:` URIs (could contain arbitrary HTML/JavaScript content)
- `filesystem:` URIs
- Custom protocol handlers

The mitigating factor is that the SanitizerFilter only applies when `RESTRICTED=true` parameter is set. However, the extra-allowed configurations (extraAllowedTags, extraAllowedAttributes, extraURISafeAttributes, extraDataUriTags) from external configuration could also open attack surfaces.

Manual review is needed to determine:
1. Whether `blob:` URIs can actually create XSS in this context
2. Whether any non-standard protocol could bypass the IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA check
3. Whether the IS_NO_URI pattern provides sufficient mitigation before reaching the allowUnknownProtocols check

evidence.code_context: isAllowUnknownProtocols() returns Boolean.TRUE as default. SecureHTMLElementSanitizer uses this at line 206 to allow URI attribute values that don't match IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA. IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA pattern = '^(?:\w+script|data):' catches javascript:, vbscript:, data:. IS_NO_URI pattern = '^(?:[^a-z]|[a-z+.\-]+(?:[^a-z+.\-:]|$))' is checked first. SanitizerFilter only applies when RESTRICTED=true.
evidence.notes: The defaulting to true for allowUnknownProtocols is counterintuitive for a 'secure' sanitizer. However, the actual impact depends on whether blob: or other protocols can create XSS. The IS_NO_URI check provides significant mitigation.

findings.description: DefaultHTMLCleaner and ScriptHTMLDiffConfiguration string constants flagged as XSS diff sanitizer config issues - false positives
findings.reasoning:
All 17 flagged locations in DefaultHTMLCleaner.java are:
1. Lines 63-111: `@Named` annotation string constants for injected HTMLFilter components ("list", "listitem", "font", "body", "attribute", "link", "controlcharacters", "sanitizer") - these are component hint names, not XSS sinks
2. Line 167: Error message string "Unhandled error when cleaning HTML" - thrown as RuntimeException, not output to HTML
3. Lines 180-181: DOCTYPE declaration strings "PUBLIC", "-//W3C//DTD XHTML 1.0 Strict//EN", "http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-strict.dtd" - standard HTML doctype identifiers
4. Line 186: Error message "Error while serializing TagNode into w3c dom." - RuntimeException message
5. Line 304: CSS value "text-align:center" - a static tag transformation for `<center>` tags
6. Line 313: Class attribute template "${class} monospace" - for `<tt>` tag transformation
7. Line 318: String "true" - boolean configuration check
8. Line 347: String "5" - HTML version number check

None of these constitute XSS vulnerabilities. They are all framework configuration constants, component registration names, static HTML transformation strings, error message texts, and HTML standard identifiers. The DefaultHTMLCleaner is itself an HTML sanitization framework component.

For ScriptHTMLDiffConfiguration.java:
- Line 42: `@Named("html/script")` component hint
- Line 50: `@Named("context")` injection hint for ComponentManager provider
- Lines 66, 83: Error message strings in exception handlers

All are false positives.

evidence.code_context: DefaultHTMLCleaner is an HTML cleaning/sanitizing framework. All flagged lines are string constants: @Named component hints, DOCTYPE declarations, static tag transformations, and error messages. ScriptHTMLDiffConfiguration flagged lines are @Named hints and logger.error() message templates.
evidence.notes: False positives - CodeQL query appears to be flagging all string literals within HTML-processing classes without proper taint analysis

findings.description: ScriptHTMLDiffConfiguration component name constants and error message strings flagged as XSS - false positives
findings.reasoning:
The flagged lines in ScriptHTMLDiffConfiguration.java are:
- Line 42: `@Named("html/script")` - component registration hint
- Line 50: `@Named("context")` - injection qualifier for ComponentManager provider
- Line 66: `logger.error("Failed to lookup StringSplitter with hint [{}].", ...)` - error log message
- Line 83: `logger.error("Failed to lookup XMLDiffFilter with hint [{}].", ...)` - error log message

None of these are XSS sinks. The error messages go to server-side logs, not HTTP responses. The @Named annotations are component framework metadata. This class is a configuration class for HTML diff computation, not an HTTP response handler.

evidence.code_context: ScriptHTMLDiffConfiguration is a configuration component for HTML diff computation. @Named annotations are component hints. logger.error() calls write to server logs, not HTTP responses. No user-controlled data reaches any HTML output context.
evidence.notes: False positive - component framework metadata and logger calls, not XSS sinks
